era_context: The Eastern Roman Empire under Emperor Leo I is in the middle of his reign (457–474 CE). The Western Roman Empire has effectively ceased to exist; Germanic kingdoms control former Roman territories. The Vandal Kingdom dominates the Mediterranean. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty is consolidating its control of northern China, while the Southern Dynasties compete for control of the south. This is a period of relative stability in the Byzantine Empire, but external threats from the Vandals and Persians are constant.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube
Central Asia and the Silk Road

events.title: Byzantine military operations and administrative consolidation under Leo I
events.description: Emperor Leo I continues to consolidate Byzantine power and authority. The year 462 falls within a period of relative stability in the Byzantine Empire, following Leo I's successful consolidation of power in the 450s. Leo I is working to strengthen the military, reform the administration, and counter external threats from the Vandals and Persians. The emperor's reliance on the Germanic general Aspar for military command is a source of tension, but it is also a practical necessity given the military challenges facing the empire.
events.certainty_note: Leo I's reign and his administrative activities are well documented. Specific events in 462 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Related to the consolidation of Byzantine power in the 450s–460s

events.title: Vandal Kingdom naval dominance and raids on Mediterranean territories
events.region: Mediterranean Sea, North Africa, Southern Europe
events.description: The Vandal Kingdom under King Gaiseric continues its naval dominance of the Mediterranean. The Vandals control North Africa (including the grain-producing provinces of Egypt and Tunisia), and their navy is the dominant force in the Mediterranean. Vandal raids on Roman territories in Sicily, southern Italy, Greece, and the Aegean islands are frequent. The year 462 falls within a period of Vandal expansion and consolidation of power. The Byzantine Empire, while militarily strong, is unable to effectively challenge Vandal naval supremacy.

sources.name: Victor of Vita, History of the Persecution of the Vandals

events.certainty_note: Vandal naval dominance in the Mediterranean is well documented for this period. Specific raids in 462 are not always individually dated.
cross_references: Part of the Vandal Kingdom's dominance of the Mediterranean (439–534)

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty: consolidation of control over northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei Dynasty, which unified northern China in 439 CE, is consolidating its control over its vast territories. The year 462 falls within a period of relative stability for the Northern Wei, before the factional strife of the 460s intensifies. Emperor Wencheng (r. 452–465) is in power and is implementing sinicization policies that attempt to integrate Han Chinese administrative practices with Xianbei military traditions. The dynasty's control over northern China is secure, but internal tensions are building.

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Northern Wei Dynasty's consolidation of control in the 460s is well documented. Specific events in 462 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Part of the Northern Wei Dynasty's consolidation (439–465)
Related to the Northern Wei's decline and fragmentation (465–534)

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed. The traditional narrative of Byzantine stability under Leo I, Vandal naval dominance, and Northern Wei consolidation are generally supported by modern scholarship.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 462 CE is similar to that of 463, 464, and 465: dominated by Byzantine and Chinese sources, with sparse Western European documentation. The year 462 is less precisely documented than 465, as it lacks the dramatic event of Leo I's death. Archaeological evidence is minimal. The bias toward political and military history means that economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Leo I's consolidation of power in 462 leads to the succession crisis of 465
Vandal naval dominance in 462 leads to the major Byzantine-Vandal War of 468
Northern Wei consolidation in 462 leads to factional strife in 465
